r ac analyses. Cir- cuits may contain resistors, capacitors, inductors, mutual induc- tors, independent voltage and current sources, four types of dependent sources, transmission lines, and the four most commo

ces, transmission lines, and the four most common semiconductor devices: diodes, BJT's, JFET's, and MOSFET's. SPICE has built-in models for the semiconductor devices, and the user need specify only the perti

1.2. AC Small-Signal Analysis The ac small-signal portion of SPICE computes the ac output variables as a function of frequency. The program first computes the dc operating point of the circuit and determ

the dc operating point of the circuit and determines linearized, small-signal models for all of the nonlinear devices in the cir- cuit. The resultant linear circuit is then analyzed over a user-specified ra

mpedance, etc). If the circuit has only one ac input, it is convenient to set that input to unity and zero phase, so that output variables have the same value as the transfer function of the output

The generation of white noise by resistors and semiconductor devices can also be simulated with the ac small-signal portion of SPICE. Equivalent noise source values are determined automati- cally from the

determined automati- cally from the small-signal operating point of the circuit, and the contribution of each noise source is added at a given summing point. The total output noise level and the equivalent in

total output noise level and the equivalent input noise level are determined at each frequency point. The output and input noise levels are normalized with respect to the square root of the noise bandwidth and
square root of the noise bandwidth and have the units Volts/rt Hz or Amps/rt Hz. The output noise and equivalent input noise can be printed or plotted in the same fashion as other output variables.

The frequency range and the noise and distortion analysis parameters are specified on the .AC, .NOISE, and .DISTO control lines.

CONVERGENCE Both dc and transient solutions are obtained by an iterative process which is terminated when both of the following conditions hold: 1) The nonlinear branch currents converge to withi
The nonlinear branch currents converge to within a tolerance of 0.1 percent or 1 picoamp (1.0E-12 Amp), whichever is larger.
8 2) The node voltages converge to within a tolerance of 0.1 per- cent or 1 microvolt (1.0E-6 Volt), whichever is larger. Although the algorithm used i
er. Although the algorithm used in SPICE has been found to be very reliable, in some cases it will fail to converge to a solu- tion. When this failure occurs, the program will print the node vol

s, the program will print the node voltages at the last iteration and terminate the job. In such cases, the node voltages that are printed are not necessarily correct or even close to the correct solution.

correct or even close to the correct solution. Failure to converge in the dc analysis is usually due to an error in specifying circuit connections, element values, or model parameter values. Regenerative swit

s used for some of the devices in the feedback path, or the .NODESET card is used to force the cir- cuit to converge to the desired state. 3. INPUT FORMAT The input format for SPICE is of the
